abstract:
This paper details the multidisciplinary investigation of the Prosiměřice 2 metal hoard, discovered in 2021 during a rescue excavation in South Moravia. Found within the Jevišovka River floodplain below the groundwater level, the assemblage includes two socketed axes, nine copper and bronze ingots, a sprue, and a metal stick. Typological analysis of the axes dates the deposit to the Late Bronze Age (Reinecke Ha B1/B2), identifying it as part of the Middle Danube Urnfield Culture. Material studies using ED-XRF reveal a diverse metallurgical profile, ranging from raw, unrefined copper to finished tin-bronze alloys, suggesting the presence of an active production chain within the local settlement area. 

Technological assessments through X-ray computed tomography and use-wear analysis indicate that while the axes were functionally prepared, they exhibit significant casting defects, such as excessive porosity and incomplete features. Environmental data, obtained through pollen analysis and historical cartography, reconstruct the site as a contemporary wetland or open water environment. By situating the find within its regional settlement hierarchy, the authors argue that the hoard reflects a local metallurgical tradition. Ultimately, the irreversible deposition of these functional yet flawed items into a watery context is interpreted as evidence of communal socio-cultic rituals, bridging the gap between economic production and symbolic sacrifice in the Bronze Age landscape.
